text: A Scrum Team has been working on a product for 9 Sprints. A new Product Owner who is new to Scrum joins the team and understands she is accountable for the Product Backlog. However, she is unsure about the purpose of the Product Backlog. She has read that the Product Backlog should be a list of all user features for the product. She goes to the Scrum Master asking where to put the other types of requirements that are going to be taken into account. Are all of the following types of requirements acceptable on a Product Backlog? Stability requirements. Performance requirements. Product Functionality. Documentation. Fixes. (choose the best answer)
options: Yes, they all belong on the Product Backlog. Product Backlog is supposed to be the Single Source of Truth for all the work for the product.

No, Product Backlog is a tool for the Product Owner. The Product Owner represents the users and stakeholders. Other types of requirements should be managed separately by the Developers. They are not the Product Owner's concern.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: Yes, they all belong on the Product Backlog. Product Backlog is supposed to be the Single Source of Truth for all the work for the product. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

explanation: Correct answer: Anything that informs them to achieve the product's goals and to optimize the value delivered. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: A high-performance Scrum Team ensures that each Increment is complete by running a Release Sprint.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: False. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: The Developers select a set of Product Backlog Items for a Sprint Backlog with the intent to get the selected items done by the end of the Sprint. Which three phrases best describe the purpose of a Definition of Done? (choose the best three answers)
options: It tracks the percentage complete of a Product Backlog Item.
It defines what it takes for an Increment to be ready for release.
It creates transparency over the work inspected at the Sprint Review.
It guides the Developers in creating a forecast at the Sprint Planning.
It provides a template for elements that need to be included in the documentation.
It controls whether the Developers have performed their tasks.
trap: Done is defined by the Definition of Done, not by acceptance or partial completion.
explanation: Correct answer: It defines what it takes for an Increment to be ready for release, It creates transparency over the work inspected at the Sprint Review, It guides the Developers in creating a forecast at the Sprint Planning. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: What are three benefits of self-management? (choose the best three answers)
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: Increased self-accountability, Increased creativity, Increased commitment. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: A new Developer is having continuing conflicts with existing members of the Scrum Team, which is impacting the delivery of the Increment. If necessary, who is responsible for removing the Developer from the Scrum Team? (choose the best answer)
options: The hiring manager is responsible, they hired the Developer.
The Product Owner is responsible, they control the return on investment (ROI).
The Scrum Master is responsible, they remove impediments.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: The Scrum Team is responsible. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: A Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product that is the result of the work of the Scrum Team. Which of the following can be delegated to others, while the Product Owner remains accountable for the work? (choose the best two answers)
options: Developing and communicating the Product Goal.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: Ordering Product Backlog Items, Developing and communicating the Product Goal. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: During a Sprint Retrospective, the Developers propose moving the Daily Scrum to only occur on Tuesdays and Thursdays. Which two are the most appropriate responses for the Scrum Master to recommend? (choose the best two answers)
options: Consider the request and decide on which days the Daily Scrum should occur.
Coach the team on why the Daily Scrum is important as an opportunity to update the plan.
Acknowledge and support the self-managing team's decision.
Learn why the Developers want this and work with them to improve the outcome of the Daily Scrum.
trap: The Daily Scrum is for Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal.
explanation: Correct answer: Coach the team on why the Daily Scrum is important as an opportunity to update the plan, Learn why the Developers want this and work with them to improve the outcome of the Daily Scrum. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: During the Sprint Retrospective a Scrum Team has identified several high priority process improvements. Which of the following statements is most accurate? (choose the best answer)
options: The Scrum Team may add the items to the Sprint Backlog for the next Sprint.
The Scrum Team should choose at least one high priority process improvement to place in the Product Backlog.
The Scrum Team should decline to add a process improvement to the Sprint Backlog when things are running smoothly.
The Scrum Master selects the most important process improvement and places it in the Sprint Backlog.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: The Scrum Team may add the items to the Sprint Backlog for the next Sprint. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: How is management that is external to the Scrum Team involved in the Daily Scrum? (choose the best answer)
options: The Product Owner represents their opinions.
Managers are not required at the Daily Scrum.
The Scrum Master speaks on their behalf.
Management gives an update at the start of each Daily Scrum.
trap: The Daily Scrum is for Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal.
explanation: Correct answer: Managers are not required at the Daily Scrum. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: How much time is required after a Sprint to prepare for the next Sprint? (choose the best answer)
options: The break between Sprints is timeboxed to one-week for a one-month Sprint, and usually less for shorter Sprints.
Enough time for the requirements for the next Sprint to be determined and documented.
Enough time for the Developers to finish the testing from the last Sprint.
None. A new Sprint starts immediately following the end of the previous Sprint.
All of the above are allowed depending on the situation.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: None. A new Sprint starts immediately following the end of the previous Sprint. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: How much work is required of the Developers to complete a Product Backlog Item selected during the Sprint Planning? (choose the best answer)
options: As much as they can fit into the Sprint, with remaining work deferred to the next Sprint.
As much as is required to meet the Scrum Team's Definition of Done.
All development work and at least some testing.
A proportional amount of time on analysis, design, development, and testing.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: As much as is required to meet the Scrum Team's Definition of Done. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: How often should Scrum Team membership change? (choose the best answer)
options: Every Sprint to promote shared learning.
As needed, with no special allowance for changes in productivity.
As needed, while taking into account a short-term reduction in productivity.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: As needed, while taking into account a short-term reduction in productivity. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: If Product Backlog Refinement is needed, when is the ideal time for refinement to take place and who should participate? (choose the best two answers)
options: Business analysts in the organization should do this work for the Scrum Team 1-2 Sprints ahead of the development Sprints.
The Product Owner must do this as essential work in Sprint 0.
The Scrum Team on an ongoing basis, defining Product Backlog Items into smaller more precise items that are ready for selection.
The Scrum Team during the current Sprint, if they have been unable during preceding Sprints to define Product Backlog Items with enough precision to begin work.
The Product Owner takes the time between the end of one Sprint and the start of the next Sprint to complete refinement.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: The Scrum Team on an ongoing basis, defining Product Backlog Items into smaller more precise items that are ready for selection, The Scrum Team during the current Sprint, if they have been unable during preceding Sprints to define Product Backlog Items with enough precision to begin work. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Scrum requires that the Product Owner must use which of the following items? (choose all that apply)
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: None of the above. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: A Product Owner with multiple teams working on one product should maintain separate Product Backlogs for each team.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: False. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: The Scrum Team is accountable for releasing the most valuable product.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: False. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: What are three advantages of a Product Owner sharing a clearly defined Product Goal with the Scrum Team? (choose the best three answers)
options: It helps the Scrum Team keep focus and they can weigh any decision against the Product Goal.
It helps the Developers estimate the date that the Product Backlog will be complete.
It is easier to inspect Incremental progress at the Sprint Review.
It is not mandatory in Scrum. There is no real advantage.
It provides a good overall direction so Sprints will feel less like isolated pieces of work.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: It helps the Scrum Team keep focus and they can weigh any decision against the Product Goal, It is easier to inspect Incremental progress at the Sprint Review, It provides a good overall direction so Sprints will feel less like isolated pieces of work. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Which approach is best for Scrum Teams in order to produce valuable Increments? (choose the best answer)
options: Each Developer works on the component where they feel that they can contribute.
Each Scrum Team is accountable for developing functionality from beginning to end.
Each Scrum Team works on an independent set of components.
Each Scrum Member works only as an independent layer of the system.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: Each Scrum Team is accountable for developing functionality from beginning to end. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Who is accountable for managing the progress of work during a Sprint? (choose the best answer)
options: The most junior member of the team.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: The Developers. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Who is part of the discussion about which Product Backlog Items are selected for a Sprint? (choose the best answer)
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: The Scrum Team. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Why does the Product Owner want the Developers to adhere to its Definition of Done? (choose the best answer)
options: To predict the teams productivity over time.
To have complete transparency into what has been done at the end of each Sprint.
To know what the team will deliver over the next three Sprints.
To be able to reprimand the team when they do not meet their velocity goal for the Sprint.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: To have complete transparency into what has been done at the end of each Sprint. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Your management has asked you to take the lead in the development of a new product. Six teams new to Scrum will build this product. You have gathered a number of requirements and ideas into an early form of a Product Backlog. How would you minimize dependencies between the Scrum Teams? (choose the best answer)
options: You work with the Developers on how to best analyze and break apart the work.
You divide Product Backlog Items among the six Product Owners.
You create an independent Product Backlog per Scrum Team.
You identify the dependencies and re-order the Product Backlog for the other five Product Owners.
You raise this as an impediment with the Scrum Master.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: You work with the Developers on how to best analyze and break apart the work. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: What does it mean to say that an event has a timebox? (choose the best answer)
options: The event must take at least a minimum amount of time.
The event must happen at a set time.
The event can take no more than a maximum amount of time.
The event must happen by a given time.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: The event can take no more than a maximum amount of time. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: A Scrum Master fulfills the same role as a traditional Project Manager.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: False. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: When is it most appropriate for a Scrum Team to change the Definition of Done? (choose the best answer)
options: Prior to starting a new project.
trap: Done is defined by the Definition of Done, not by acceptance or partial completion.
explanation: Correct answer: During the Sprint Retrospective. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: A Product Owner should measure product value by the increase in the team's velocity.
trap: Velocity is not a measure of product value or cross-team performance.
explanation: Correct answer: False. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Why do the Developers need a Sprint Goal? (choose the best answer)
options: The Developers are more focused with a common yet specific goal.
Sprint Goals are not valuable. Everything is known from the Product Backlog.
A Sprint Goal only gives purpose to Sprint 0.
Sprint Goal ensures that all of the Product Backlog Items selected for the Sprint are implemented.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: The Developers are more focused with a common yet specific goal. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Who creates a Product Backlog Item's estimate? (choose the best answer)
options: The Product Owner with input from the Developers.
The Developers after clarifying requirements with the Product Owner.
The most senior people in the organization, including architects and subject matter experts.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: The Developers after clarifying requirements with the Product Owner. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: Every Scrum Team must have a Product Owner and a Scrum Master.
options: False, a Scrum Master is only required when asked for by the Developers.
True, outcomes are affected by their participation and availability.
True, each must be 100% dedicated to their Scrum Team.
False, a Product Owner can be replaced by a Business Analyst among Developer.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: True, outcomes are affected by their participation and availability. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Which topics should be discussed in the Sprint Review? (choose the best answer)
options: The Scrum process, and how it was used during the Sprint.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: The product Increment. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Five new Scrum Teams have been created to build one product. A few of the Developers on one of the Scrum Teams ask the Scrum Master who will coordinate the work with the other teams. What should the Scrum Master do? (choose the best answer)
options: Collect the Sprint tasks from the teams at the end of their Sprint Planning and merge that into a consolidated plan for the entire Sprint.
Teach them that it is their responsibility to work with the other teams to create an Increment that is inclusive of all five team's work.
Teach the Product Owner to work with the Lead Developer on ordering Product Backlog in a way to avoid too much overlap during a Sprint.
Visit the five teams each day to inspect that their Sprint Backlogs are aligned.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: Teach them that it is their responsibility to work with the other teams to create an Increment that is inclusive of all five team's work. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: Cross-functional teams are optimized to work on one component or layer of a system only.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: False. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: What are two responsibilities of Testers in a Scrum Team? (choose the best two answers)
options: The Developers are responsible for quality.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: Scrum has no "Tester" role, The Developers are responsible for quality. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Which two ways of creating Scrum Teams are consistent with Scrum's values? (choose the best two answers)
options: Existing teams propose how they would like to go about organizing into the new structure.
Bring all people together and let them organize into Scrum Teams.
Managers personally re-assign current subordinates to new teams.
The chief Product Owner determines the new team structure and assignments.
Managers collaborate to assign individuals into specific teams.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: Existing teams propose how they would like to go about organizing into the new structure, Bring all people together and let them organize into Scrum Teams. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: The Scrum Team is accountable for creating a valuable Increment every Sprint.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: True. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: When does the next Sprint begin? (choose the best answer)
options: The Monday following the Sprint Review.
Immediately after the conclusion of the previous Sprint.
Immediately following the next Sprint Planning.
When the Product Owner is ready.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: Immediately after the conclusion of the previous Sprint. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: An Increment must be released to customers or users at the end of each Sprint.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: False. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: What is the accountability of the Product Owner during Sprint 0? (choose the best answer)
options: There is no such thing as Sprint 0.
Create the overall release plan to ensure that organizational goals are met.
Determine the composition of the Scrum Team to meet the release plan.
Gathering, eliciting and analyzing the requirements to build the Product Backlog.
Make sure enough Product backlog items are refined to fill the first 3 Sprints.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: There is no such thing as Sprint 0. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: True or False: When there are multiple teams working on one product, each Scrum Team should have a separate Product Owner.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: False. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Which answer best describes the topics covered in Sprint Planning? (choose the best answer)
options: Who is the team and what team member roles will be.
What to do and who will do it.
What can be done, how to do it, and why to do it.
What went wrong in the last Sprint and what to do differently this Sprint.
How conditions have changed and how the Product Backlog should evolve.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: What can be done, how to do it, and why to do it. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Which of the following might the Scrum Team discuss during a Sprint Retrospective? (choose the best answer)
options: The way the Scrum Team does Sprint Planning.
Skills needed to improve the Scrum Teams ability to deliver.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: All of the above. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: An organization has decided to adopt Scrum, but management wants to change the terminology to fit with terminology already used. What will likely happen if this is done? (choose the best answer)
options: Without a new vocabulary as a reminder of the change, very little change may actually happen.
The organization may not understand what has changed with Scrum and the benefits of Scrum may be lost.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: All of the above. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Which three of the following are feedback loops in Scrum? (choose the best three answers)
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: Sprint Retrospective, Sprint Review, Daily Scrum. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: The IT manager asks the Scrum Team for a status report describing the progress throughout the Sprint. The Scrum Team ask the Scrum Master for advice. The Scrum Master should: (choose the best answer)
options: Create and deliver the report to the manager herself.
Tell the Developers to figure it out themselves.
Tell the Developers to fit the report into the Sprint Backlog.
Talk to the IT manager and explain that the progress in Scrum comes from inspecting an Increment at the Sprint Review.
Ask the Product Owner to send the manager the report.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: Talk to the IT manager and explain that the progress in Scrum comes from inspecting an Increment at the Sprint Review. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: What happens if the Scrum Team cannot complete its work by the end of the Sprint? (choose the best answer)
options: The Sprint length is unchanged and the Scrum Team continuously learns and adapts.
The Sprint is extended temporarily. Lessons are taken to ensure it does not happen again.
The Sprint is extended and future Sprints use this new duration.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: The Sprint length is unchanged and the Scrum Team continuously learns and adapts. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: User documentation is part of your Definition of Done. However, there are not enough Technical Writers for all teams. Your Scrum Team does not have a Technical Writer. What should the Scrum Team do? (choose the best answer)
options: Wait until you have a Technical Writer on your Scrum Team to take care of this.
Let the user documentation remain undone and accumulate until after the last development Sprint. It will then be done by any available Technical Writers.
The Developers on the Scrum Team should write the user documentation.
Form a separate team of Technical Writers that will work on an on-demand basis for the various Product Owners. Work order will be first in, first out.
trap: Done is defined by the Definition of Done, not by acceptance or partial completion.
explanation: Correct answer: The Developers on the Scrum Team should write the user documentation. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: The Scrum Master observes the Product Owner is struggling with ordering the Product Backlog. What is an appropriate action for the Scrum Master to take? (choose the best answer)
options: Present the Product Owner with an ordered Product Backlog to use.
Suggest that the Developers order the Product Backlog to be sure that it is a feasible ordering of work.
Offer the Product Owner help in understanding that the goal of ordering the Product Backlog is to maximize value.
Encourage the Product Owner to work with the Developers to see which items technically are fastest to implement.
Suggest the Product Owner extend the Sprint, so he can have more time to order the Product Backlog.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: Offer the Product Owner help in understanding that the goal of ordering the Product Backlog is to maximize value. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: When must the Product Owner be present at the Daily Scrum? (choose the best answer)
options: When they need to represent the stakeholders' point of view.
When the Scrum Master asks them to attend.
When there are impediments to discuss.
When the Product Owner is actively working on items from the Sprint Backlog.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: When the Product Owner is actively working on items from the Sprint Backlog. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: How much of the Sprint Backlog must be defined during the Sprint Planning event? (choose the best answer)
options: Just enough to understand design and architectural implications.
Just enough tasks for the Scrum Master to be confident in the Developers' understanding of the Sprint.
The entire Sprint Backlog must be identified and estimated by the end of Sprint Planning.
Enough so the Developers can create their forecast of what work they can do.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: Enough so the Developers can create their forecast of what work they can do. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: You are the Scrum Master of a new, to be developed Product. Development is going to require 45 people. What is a good first question for you to suggest the group thinks about when forming into teams? (choose the best answer)
options: What is the right mixture of senior and junior people on each team?
How will we make sure all teams have the right amount of expertise?
Who are the subject matter experts on each team?
Who are going to be the team leads?
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: How will we make sure all teams have the right amount of expertise?. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: What activities would a Product Owner typically undertake in the period between the end of the current Sprint and the start of the next Sprint? (choose the best answer)
options: There are no such activities, the next Sprint starts immediately after the current Sprint.
Update the project plan with stakeholders.
Work with the Quality Assurance departments on the Increment of the current Sprint.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: There are no such activities, the next Sprint starts immediately after the current Sprint. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Several Sprints into a project, the Product Owner tells the Scrum Master that a key stakeholder just started using the product. The stakeholder is unhappy with the quality of the product. What are two good options for the Scrum Master? (choose the best two answers)
options: Explain to the Product Owner that it is up to the Developers to decide on acceptable quality standards.
Encourage the Product Owner to put quality specifications on the Product Backlog and express the stakeholder's concern to the Developers.
Coach the Product Owner on how to talk with the Developers about this concern.
Bring the concern to the Testers to improve how the Product is verified.
Wait to bring this up until the Sprint Retrospective.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: Encourage the Product Owner to put quality specifications on the Product Backlog and express the stakeholder's concern to the Developers, Coach the Product Owner on how to talk with the Developers about this concern. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: As the Developers start work during the Sprint, they realize they have selected too much work to finish the Sprint. What should they do? (choose the best answer)
options: Inform the Product Owner at the Sprint review, but prior to the demonstration.
Find another Scrum Team to give the excess work to.
Reduce the Definition of Done and get all of the Product Backlog Items done by the new definition.
As soon as possible in the Sprint, work with the Product Owner to remove some work or Product Backlog Items.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: As soon as possible in the Sprint, work with the Product Owner to remove some work or Product Backlog Items. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Who does the work to make sure Product Backlog Items conform to the Definition of Done? (choose the best answer)
trap: Done is defined by the Definition of Done, not by acceptance or partial completion.
explanation: Correct answer: The Developers. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Which two things are appropriate for a Scrum Master to do if the Scrum Team does not have the tools and environment to completely finish each selected Product Backlog Item? (choose the best two answers)
options: Encourage the Product Owner to accept partially done Increments until the situation improves.
Have the Scrum Team establish a Definition of Done that is actually possible to achieve given current circumstances.
Declare the Scrum Team not ready for Scrum.
Refocus the current Sprint on establishing the Scrum Team's environment instead of delivering an Increment.
Coach the Scrum Team to improve its skills: tools and environment over time and adjust the Definition of Done accordingly.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: Have the Scrum Team establish a Definition of Done that is actually possible to achieve given current circumstances, Coach the Scrum Team to improve its skills: tools and environment over time and adjust the Definition of Done accordingly. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: For the purpose of transparency, when does Scrum say a valuable and useful Increment must be available? (choose the best answer)
options: When the Product Owner asks to create one.
At the end of every Sprint.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: At the end of every Sprint. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Who is accountable for clearly expressing Product Backlog Items? (choose the best answer)
options: The Business Analyst who represents the Product Owner.
The Scrum Master, or the Scrum Master may have the Developers do it.
trap: Avoid applying traditional project management assumptions to Scrum.
explanation: Correct answer: The Product Owner. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Which statement best describes the Sprint Review? (choose the best answer)
options: It is a mechanism to control the Developers activities during a Sprint.
It is used to congratulate the Developers if they complete their forecast or to punish the Developers if they fail to meet their forecast.
It is when the Scrum Team and stakeholders inspect the outcome of a Sprint and figure out what to do next.
It is a demo at the end of the Sprint for everyone in the organization to check on the work done

explanation: Correct answer: 8 hours for a one-month Sprint. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Who owns the Sprint Backlog? (choose the best answer)
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: The Developers. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Which of the following are appropriate topics for discussion during a Sprint Retrospective? (choose the best two answers)
options: Documenting acceptance criteria for items in the next Sprint.
The order of items in the Product Backlog.
Identifying high priority process improvements for the next Sprint.
trap: Scrum protects the Sprint Goal and the timebox; it does not guarantee all forecast work.
explanation: Correct answer: Identifying high priority process improvements for the next Sprint, How the team collaborates. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: What is the Product Owner responsible for during the Sprint Retrospective? (choose the best answer)
options: Participating as a Scrum Team member.
The Product Owner should not take part in Sprint Retrospective.
Capturing requirements for the Product Backlog.
Summarizing and reporting the discussions to the stakeholders that they represent in the Scrum Team.
trap: Do not confuse Product Owner accountability with stakeholder or Developer decisions.
explanation: Correct answer: Participating as a Scrum Team member. This aligns with Scrum Guide principles around accountability, transparency, empiricism, and value delivery.

text: Who is accountable for making Product Backlog ordering decisions visible?
trap: The Product Owner owns Product Backlog ordering.
explanation: The Product Owner is accountable for effective Product Backlog management, including ordering.

text: What should a Product Owner do when product usage data contradicts stakeholder opinions?
options: Ignore the data because stakeholders own business value
Inspect the evidence and use it to adapt Product Backlog ordering
Ask Developers to decide which stakeholder is right
trap: Stakeholder opinions are input, not the sole source of truth.
explanation: Scrum relies on empiricism; evidence should inform product decisions and Product Backlog ordering.

text: SCENARIO: A Product Owner receives conflicting requests from Sales, Support, and Compliance. Which actions are consistent with Scrum? (Select all that apply)
options: Order the Product Backlog based on overall product value and risk
Make the decision transparent through Product Backlog ordering
Collaborate with stakeholders to understand trade-offs
trap: Stakeholders influence but do not vote the backlog order.
explanation: The Product Owner considers input and remains accountable for Product Backlog decisions.

text: Which statement best describes Product Backlog refinement?
options: It is a mandatory Scrum event
It is an ongoing activity that adds detail, order, and estimates
It is a phase before Sprint 1
It is done only by the Scrum Master
trap: Refinement is not a formal Scrum event.
explanation: Refinement is an ongoing activity performed as needed by the Scrum Team.

text: Which information can help refine a Product Backlog Item? (Select all that apply)
options: The Scrum Master assigning the item to a Developer
trap: Assignment to individuals is not refinement.
explanation: Useful refinement improves shared understanding of value, feasibility, dependencies, and size.

text: What is Scrum primarily designed for?
options: Managing simple repetitive work with fixed steps
Generating value through adaptive solutions for complex problems
trap: Scrum is not a guarantee or full methodology.
explanation: Scrum is a lightweight framework for complex work based on empiricism and lean thinking.

text: What does empiricism require in Scrum?
options: Predictions based only on upfront planning
Decisions based on observation and evidence
A project manager to approve changes
trap: Empiricism is evidence-based.
explanation: Scrum decisions are based on what is known through transparency, inspection, and adaptation.

text: Who decides how Developers organize their work during a Sprint?
trap: Self-management belongs to Developers.
explanation: Developers are accountable for creating a plan and managing their Sprint Backlog.

text: Which statements about Developers are true? (Select all that apply)
options: They are accountable for creating a usable Increment
They decide how to turn selected work into an Increment
They are assigned tasks by the Product Owner
trap: Developers are self-managing; tasks are not assigned by the PO.
explanation: Developers own the Sprint Backlog, quality, and the work needed to create Done Increments.

text: What is the main purpose of the Daily Scrum?
options: To report status to the Scrum Master
To inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog
trap: Daily Scrum is not a status meeting.
explanation: The Daily Scrum helps Developers inspect progress and adapt their plan.

text: What should happen during Sprint Review?
options: The Scrum Team and stakeholders inspect outcomes and adapt what to do next
The Product Owner formally accepts or rejects all work
The Scrum Master evaluates Developer performance
trap: Sprint Review is not an acceptance gate.
explanation: The Sprint Review is a working session to inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog.

text: When is work considered part of an Increment?
options: When the Product Owner likes it
When it meets the Definition of Done
When development is complete but testing remains
trap: Done is defined by the DoD.
explanation: Only work that meets the Definition of Done is part of an Increment.

text: Which statements about the Definition of Done are correct? (Select all that apply)
options: It describes the required quality state of the Increment
It can be ignored for urgent releases
trap: Urgency does not justify ignoring Done.
explanation: The Definition of Done is a formal description of quality and supports transparency and forecasting.

text: What is the commitment for the Product Backlog?
trap: Each artifact has one commitment.
explanation: The Product Goal is the commitment associated with the Product Backlog.

text: What is the purpose of a Sprint Goal?
options: To provide a single objective and focus for the Sprint
To list every task Developers must do
trap: Sprint Goal is not a task list.
explanation: The Sprint Goal provides focus and flexibility for the Developers.

text: SCENARIO: A team reports items as Done even though integration testing is postponed to the next Sprint. Which statements are correct? (Select all that apply)
options: The items should not be considered Done
The Definition of Done should be respected
Velocity should include the partial work
trap: Partial work should not inflate progress.
explanation: Undone work cannot be counted as Done or included in the Increment.

text: When several Scrum Teams work on one product, how many Product Backlogs should there be?
options: One Product Backlog for the product
One Product Backlog per stakeholder group
One technical backlog and one business backlog
trap: One product has one Product Backlog.
explanation: Multiple Scrum Teams working on one product share one Product Backlog and one Product Owner.

text: SCENARIO: Three Scrum Teams work on the same product. At Sprint Review, their work cannot be integrated. What concerns should be raised? (Select all that apply)
options: The Increment may not be transparent
The teams may be unable to inspect the real product state
A hardening Sprint is required by Scrum
trap: Hardening Sprints are not Scrum events.
explanation: Integrated Done work is needed for transparency and inspection.
